_meta.purpose: General drug-handbook cards for the Drug Guide screen (ward/emergency medications beyond the titration drips).
_meta.sourcing: Grounded in OPEN, freely available references: U.S. FDA prescribing information (DailyMed / openFDA), Open RN 'Nursing Pharmacology' (CC BY), StatPearls, MedlinePlus, and the WHO Model Formulary; AHA ACLS guidelines for the emergency drugs. Dose summaries and antidotes spot-verified against these sources (2025-2026) and are typical adult references for learning. PARAPHRASED nursing implications — never verbatim monograph text. The prescriber's order and facility policy always override; pediatric dosing defers to a pediatric reference.
_meta.selection: High-yield medications for nursing students and ward nurses, organized by drug class. Spans ACLS/emergency, cardiovascular, antibiotics, endocrine/antidiabetics, anticoagulants, pain/sedation + reversal, respiratory, GI, CNS/seizure, fluids & electrolytes, and obstetrics.

drugs.doseSummary: Symptomatic bradycardia: 0.5–1 mg IV q3–5 min, max 3 mg (ACLS)
keyActions: Rapid IV push (≥0.5 mg to avoid paradoxical bradycardia)
Expect dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention

nursing.before: Baseline HR, rhythm, BP. Continuous ECG during use.
nursing.administration: Rapid IV push; doses under 0.5 mg in adults can cause paradoxical bradycardia.
nursing.monitor: HR/rhythm response, anticholinergic effects — dry mouth, urinary retention, blurred vision, confusion (elderly).
nursing.holdNotify: Tachydysrhythmias, chest pain, worsening rhythm.
nursing.contraindication: Angle-closure glaucoma; use caution in MI (tachycardia raises O2 demand).
nursing.teaching: Dry mouth and blurred vision are expected and transient.
nursing.source: per AHA ACLS guidelines & FDA labeling (DailyMed)

drugs.doseSummary: Stable SVT: 6 mg rapid IV push, then 12 mg if no conversion (ACLS)
keyActions: Push fast over 1–2 s, then immediate 20 mL NS flush
Warn patient first: brief chest pressure/flushing
Continuous ECG — a brief pause is expected

nursing.before: Continuous ECG and emergency equipment at bedside. Large-bore IV as proximal as possible (antecubital).
nursing.administration: Push over 1–2 seconds, immediately followed by a 20 mL NS flush; half-life is seconds.
nursing.monitor: ECG during push — a brief pause/asystole is expected before conversion. BP, HR.
nursing.holdNotify: Prolonged asystole, bronchospasm, persistent dysrhythmia.
nursing.teaching: Warn the patient FIRST: brief chest pressure, flushing, and a frightening 'sinking' feeling lasting seconds are expected.
nursing.contraindication: 2nd/3rd-degree block or sick sinus without pacemaker; caution in asthma.
nursing.source: per AHA ACLS guidelines & FDA labeling (DailyMed)

drugs.doseSummary: Maintenance 0.125–0.25 mg PO/IV daily; therapeutic level 0.5–2 ng/mL
keyActions: Apical pulse 1 full minute — hold if <60 (adult)
Check K+ (low K+ → toxicity) & dig level
Watch toxicity: nausea, visual halos; antidote DigiFab

nursing.before: Apical pulse for one FULL minute — hold and notify if <60/min in adults (peds threshold per pediatric reference). Check potassium: hypokalemia potentiates toxicity.
nursing.monitor: Digoxin level, K+/Mg2+/Ca2+, renal function, HR and rhythm.
nursing.holdNotify: HR below cutoff, or toxicity signs: anorexia, nausea/vomiting (earliest), visual changes — halos, yellow-green tint — confusion, new dysrhythmias.
nursing.teaching: Take the pulse before each dose; same time daily; report appetite loss or visual changes immediately.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 20–80 mg PO/IV; IV push slowly (≤20 mg/min) — rapid push risks ototoxicity
keyActions: Monitor K+, BP, strict I&O, daily weight
Watch hearing (ototoxicity); dose in the morning

nursing.before: Baseline BP, K+, renal function, weight.
nursing.administration: Give in the morning (and early afternoon if BID) to avoid nocturia. Slow IV push.
nursing.monitor: K+ (hypokalemia — watch with digoxin!), BP (orthostatic), strict I&O, daily weight, hearing (tinnitus = ototoxicity).
nursing.holdNotify: SBP below ordered floor, K+ below normal, no urine response, hearing changes.
nursing.teaching: Rise slowly; eat potassium-rich foods unless restricted; report ringing in ears; daily same-scale weights.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 25–100 mg PO BID (tartrate); IV 5 mg slow push per protocol
keyActions: Apical pulse & BP — hold if HR<60 or SBP<100

nursing.before: Apical pulse and BP — hold and clarify if HR <60 or SBP <100 (or per order/policy).
nursing.monitor: HR, BP, signs of HF (edema, dyspnea, weight gain), glucose in diabetics — beta blockade masks tachycardia of hypoglycemia.
nursing.holdNotify: Bradycardia, hypotension, wheezing (bronchospasm), worsening HF.
nursing.teaching: Never stop abruptly — taper (rebound hypertension/angina). Check pulse before doses. Diabetics: monitor glucose more closely.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 12.5–50 mg PO BID–TID, 1 hour before meals
keyActions: Empty stomach; give first dose lying down

nursing.before: Baseline BP — first-dose hypotension is common; give the first dose with the patient lying down if possible.
nursing.administration: Empty stomach (food reduces absorption).
nursing.monitor: BP, K+ (hyperkalemia — no salt substitutes), renal function, persistent dry cough, angioedema (face/lip/tongue swelling = emergency).
nursing.holdNotify: SBP below ordered floor, K+ elevated, any facial/airway swelling.
nursing.contraindication: Pregnancy (fetal injury), history of ACE-inhibitor angioedema, bilateral renal artery stenosis.
nursing.teaching: Report a nagging dry cough (may need switch), swelling of lips/face immediately; avoid potassium salt substitutes.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 2.5–10 mg PO once daily
keyActions: Rise slowly; do not stop abruptly

nursing.monitor: BP and HR, peripheral edema (common, dose-related), dizziness, headache.
nursing.holdNotify: Symptomatic hypotension, worsening edema with dyspnea.
nursing.teaching: Edema of ankles is common — report if marked; rise slowly; do not stop abruptly; good oral hygiene (gingival hyperplasia is possible with CCBs).
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: ACS: 162–325 mg chewed (non–enteric-coated); prophylaxis 75–100 mg daily
keyActions: Suspected ACS: have patient CHEW non–enteric-coated
Take the daily dose with food

nursing.administration: For suspected ACS, have the patient CHEW non–enteric-coated aspirin for fast absorption.
nursing.monitor: Bleeding (gums, stool, urine, bruising), GI pain, tinnitus (salicylate toxicity sign).
nursing.holdNotify: Active bleeding, melena, ringing in the ears.
nursing.contraindication: Children/teens with viral illness (Reye's syndrome), active GI bleed, aspirin allergy/asthma triad.
nursing.teaching: Take with food for daily use; report black stools; stop per surgeon's instruction before procedures.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 2–10 mg PO daily, individualized to INR (usual target 2–3)
keyActions: Bleeding precautions; keep vitamin K intake consistent

nursing.before: Baseline PT/INR; review for interactions — warfarin interacts with very many drugs and foods.
nursing.monitor: INR per protocol, bleeding precautions (soft toothbrush, electric razor), Hgb/Hct, occult blood.
nursing.holdNotify: INR above target range, any active bleeding, falls/head injury.
nursing.antidote: Vitamin K (phytonadione); severe bleeding per protocol (PCC/FFP)
nursing.teaching: Keep vitamin K intake CONSISTENT (don't binge or eliminate leafy greens); same time daily; regular INR checks; medical-alert ID; report blood in urine/stool, unusual bruising.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Prophylaxis 40 mg subcut daily; treatment 1 mg/kg subcut q12h (renal-adjusted)
keyActions: Deep subcut in abdomen — don't expel air bubble or rub

nursing.administration: Deep subcut into the abdominal 'love handles' (anterolateral wall), alternate sides; do NOT expel the prefilled air bubble; do NOT rub the site; do not aspirate.
nursing.monitor: Bleeding, platelets (HIT), anti-Xa level if ordered (renal impairment, obesity, pregnancy); renal function.
nursing.holdNotify: Active bleeding, platelet drop >50%, epidural catheter timing per policy (spinal hematoma risk).
nursing.teaching: Bruising at injection sites is common; report unusual bleeding; self-injection technique if going home on it.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 2–10 mg IV slow push (over 4–5 min) q3–4h PRN, individualized
keyActions: Check RR & sedation — hold if RR<12 or oversedated

nursing.before: Pain score, respiratory rate and sedation level — hold and notify if RR <12 or oversedated; baseline BP.
nursing.administration: Dilute and push slowly; have naloxone and resuscitation equipment available.
nursing.monitor: RR, sedation scale (sedation precedes respiratory depression), BP (hypotension), pain relief, urinary retention, constipation (give prescribed bowel regimen).
nursing.holdNotify: RR <12, difficult to rouse, SBP below ordered floor.
nursing.teaching: Call for help before getting up (dizziness/falls); report nausea or itching; constipation prevention.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 0.4–2 mg IV/IM/intranasal, repeat q2–3 min as needed
keyActions: Titrate to breathing, not full arousal

nursing.administration: Titrate to adequate breathing — full reversal also reverses analgesia and can precipitate acute withdrawal.
nursing.monitor: RR and sedation CONTINUOUSLY after response — naloxone is shorter-acting than most opioids, so re-sedation is common; repeat dosing may be needed. BP, HR, withdrawal signs.
nursing.holdNotify: Re-sedation, RR dropping again, agitation/withdrawal.
nursing.teaching: Family/caregiver: the patient must still be observed after waking — the overdose can outlast the antidote.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Status epilepticus: 5–10 mg IV slow (≤5 mg/min), may repeat per protocol
keyActions: Slow IV into a large vein; don't mix in the syringe

nursing.administration: Inject slowly into a large vein (phlebitis/tissue irritation); do NOT mix with other drugs in the same syringe; IM absorption is erratic.
nursing.monitor: RR and airway (respiratory depression, worse with opioids/alcohol), BP, sedation level, seizure activity.
nursing.holdNotify: RR <12, hard to rouse, hypotension.
nursing.antidote: Flumazenil (caution: can provoke seizures in dependent patients)
nursing.teaching: No driving; avoid alcohol; fall precautions; do not stop long-term use abruptly.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 500 mg–1 g PO/IV q4–6h; max 4 g/day adult (lower with hepatic risk/alcohol use)
keyActions: Count ALL sources — max 4 g/day adult

nursing.administration: COUNT ALL SOURCES — many combination cold/pain products contain paracetamol; the daily max is cumulative.
nursing.monitor: Temperature/pain response; hepatic function with prolonged or high-dose use.
nursing.holdNotify: Signs of hepatotoxicity: RUQ pain, jaundice, dark urine.
nursing.teaching: Never exceed the daily maximum; check labels of other medicines for hidden paracetamol; limit alcohol.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 200–400 mg PO q4–6h with food; max 1.2 g/day OTC (higher per prescriber)
keyActions: Watch GI bleeding (black stools) & urine output

nursing.administration: Give with food or milk to reduce GI upset.
nursing.monitor: GI bleeding (black stools, epigastric pain), renal function/urine output (especially elderly, dehydrated, or on ACE inhibitors/diuretics), BP, edema.
nursing.holdNotify: GI bleeding signs, oliguria, worsening BP control.
nursing.contraindication: Active GI bleed/ulcer, third-trimester pregnancy, aspirin-sensitive asthma; caution in cardiac and renal disease.
nursing.teaching: Take with food; report black stools; avoid stacking with other NSAIDs; hydrate.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Load per protocol; maintenance 300–400 mg/day; level 10–20 mcg/mL
keyActions: IV in NS only, ≤50 mg/min, with inline filter
Oral hygiene; never stop abruptly; monitor level

nursing.administration: IV: NS ONLY (precipitates in dextrose), ≤50 mg/min, large vein with inline filter — extravasation causes severe injury (purple glove syndrome). Flush before/after.
nursing.monitor: Phenytoin level, ECG/BP during IV loading, gait/nystagmus/ataxia (toxicity), gum changes, rash (stop if severe — SJS risk).
nursing.holdNotify: Toxicity signs, bradycardia/hypotension during infusion, rash.
nursing.teaching: Meticulous oral hygiene and dental visits (gingival hyperplasia); never stop abruptly (status epilepticus); reduces hormonal contraceptive effectiveness; same brand/timing daily.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 0.25–1 g/kg IV over 30–60 min for raised ICP, per protocol
keyActions: Use an inline filter (crystallizes); strict I&O

nursing.before: Inspect the solution — mannitol crystallizes; warm to dissolve and ALWAYS use an inline filter. Baseline neuro status, electrolytes, osmolality. Insert urinary catheter for strict I&O.
nursing.monitor: Neuro status/ICP response, strict hourly I&O, serum osmolality and electrolytes, lungs — can precipitate pulmonary edema/HF as it pulls fluid intravascularly.
nursing.holdNotify: Anuria/no diuretic response, crackles/dyspnea, osmolality above protocol ceiling.
nursing.contraindication: Anuria, severe dehydration, active intracranial bleeding (relative, per order).
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Neb 2.5 mg PRN/q4–6h; MDI 1–2 puffs q4–6h PRN (rescue)
keyActions: Give before an inhaled steroid; use a spacer
Assess lung sounds/peak flow before & after
Watch HR, tremor, K+ with frequent nebs

nursing.administration: If used with an inhaled corticosteroid, give salbutamol FIRST (opens airways), wait, then the steroid. Use a spacer with MDIs.
nursing.monitor: Lung sounds and peak flow before/after, HR (tachycardia), tremor, potassium with frequent/continuous nebs (drives K+ into cells).
nursing.holdNotify: Paradoxical bronchospasm, chest pain, marked tachycardia.
nursing.teaching: This is the RESCUE inhaler — carry it always; correct MDI-spacer technique; increasing use means worsening control — see the provider.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 100 mg IV q8h (stress dosing/adrenal crisis per protocol); PO doses vary
keyActions: Never stop long-term steroids abruptly — taper

nursing.administration: IV push slowly or infuse per label; give PO doses with food; morning dosing mimics diurnal rhythm.
nursing.monitor: Blood glucose (hyperglycemia — including non-diabetics), BP, K+ (hypokalemia), signs of infection (may be MASKED — fever may be absent), GI bleeding, mood changes.
nursing.holdNotify: Glucose persistently elevated, GI bleeding signs, signs of infection.
nursing.teaching: NEVER stop long-term steroids abruptly (adrenal crisis) — always taper; take with food; report black stools; expect possible mood/sleep changes; medical-alert ID for chronic use.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 1–2 g IV/IM q24h (q12h for meningitis per protocol)

nursing.before: Allergy history — cross-reactivity with penicillin allergy is low but real; clarify severe penicillin reactions. Obtain cultures before the first dose when ordered.
nursing.administration: NEVER mix or co-infuse with calcium-containing solutions (fatal precipitates in neonates; separate lines/flush in others per policy). IM: dilute per policy (lidocaine diluent is common — verify).
nursing.monitor: Hypersensitivity, IV site, diarrhea — watch for C. difficile (profuse/watery), superinfection (thrush).
nursing.holdNotify: Rash/anaphylaxis, severe diarrhea.
nursing.teaching: Complete the full course; report watery diarrhea even after finishing.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 15–20 mg/kg IV q8–12h, adjusted to levels (trough/AUC per policy)
keyActions: Infuse over ≥60 min (slow for red-man flushing)

nursing.administration: Infuse over AT LEAST 60 minutes — rapid infusion causes vancomycin flushing ('red man') syndrome: flushing, pruritus, hypotension. Slow the rate if it occurs.
nursing.monitor: Trough level before the dose per policy, renal function (creatinine, urine output — nephrotoxic), hearing/tinnitus (ototoxic), IV site (irritant).
nursing.holdNotify: Rising creatinine, hearing changes, trough above target.
nursing.teaching: Report ringing in the ears or decreased hearing immediately.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Conventional 1–2.5 mg/kg q8h or extended-interval 5–7 mg/kg q24h, by levels
keyActions: Watch ototoxicity (tinnitus, vertigo) — may be permanent

nursing.before: Baseline renal function and hearing assessment; weight for dosing.
nursing.monitor: Peak and trough levels per policy, creatinine and urine output (nephrotoxicity — usually reversible), tinnitus/vertigo/hearing loss (ototoxicity — often IRREVERSIBLE), vestibular signs.
nursing.holdNotify: Trough elevated, creatinine rising, any hearing/balance changes.
nursing.teaching: Report ringing ears, dizziness, or fullness in the head immediately; keep hydrated.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 10 mg PO/IV q6–8h, 30 min before meals and at bedtime
keyActions: Give 30 min before meals; slow IV push

nursing.administration: Give 30 minutes before meals (prokinetic effect). IV push slowly over 1–2 min (rapid push causes intense restlessness).
nursing.monitor: Extrapyramidal symptoms — restlessness (akathisia), acute dystonia (face/neck spasms), and with use >12 weeks, tardive dyskinesia (boxed warning). Drowsiness.
nursing.holdNotify: Any involuntary movements, severe restlessness.
nursing.contraindication: GI obstruction, perforation, or hemorrhage; Parkinson's disease; seizure disorder.
nursing.teaching: Report uncontrollable movements or restlessness immediately; avoid driving until effects known.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 20–40 mg PO once daily, 30–60 min before breakfast
keyActions: Long-term: monitor Mg/B12; watch C. diff diarrhea

nursing.administration: Swallow delayed-release capsules whole — do not crush or chew (capsule contents may be sprinkled on soft food per label if needed).
nursing.monitor: Symptom relief; with long-term use: magnesium level, B12, fracture risk, C. difficile-associated diarrhea.
nursing.holdNotify: Severe diarrhea, signs of hypomagnesemia (tremor, cramps, dysrhythmia).
nursing.teaching: Take before the first meal; long-term use only under provider guidance; report watery diarrhea.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: IV: diluted and pump-controlled ONLY, commonly ≤10 mEq/hr peripherally per policy; PO with food and a full glass of water
keyActions: NEVER IV push — always diluted on a pump
Verify urine output first; ≤10 mEq/hr peripheral per policy

nursing.before: Verify urine output before giving (risk of hyperkalemia in oliguria/renal failure). Verify dilution and rate against policy.
nursing.administration: NEVER IV push — fatal. Always diluted, always on an infusion pump; higher concentrations/rates need a central line and ECG monitoring per policy. KCl is a vesicant-like irritant — check the IV site frequently.
nursing.monitor: Serum K+, ECG with higher rates, IV site (burning — slow the rate per policy), urine output.
nursing.holdNotify: Oliguria, K+ above range, ECG changes (peaked T waves), site pain/infiltration.
nursing.teaching: Take oral K+ with food and water, sitting upright; do not crush extended-release tablets; don't use salt substitutes on top of supplements.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 1–2 g (10–20 mL of 10%) IV slow, per protocol
keyActions: Slow IV via a large vein; ECG during
Antidote for magnesium toxicity — keep at bedside

nursing.administration: IV slowly (rapid push → bradycardia/arrest); preferably via large vein — extravasation causes tissue necrosis. Do NOT mix with ceftriaxone or phosphate-containing solutions (precipitates).
nursing.monitor: ECG during IV administration, calcium level, IV site closely, BP.
nursing.holdNotify: Bradycardia, dysrhythmia, site pain/swelling.
nursing.teaching: Used as the antidote for magnesium sulfate toxicity and for cardiac protection in hyperkalemia — keep at bedside during mag infusions per protocol.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Induction: low-dose titration via pump per protocol; postpartum hemorrhage: 10–40 units in 1 L per protocol
keyActions: IV piggyback on a pump; never leave unmonitored
Continuous FHR + contraction pattern & resting tone
STOP for tachysystole or non-reassuring FHR

nursing.before: Baseline maternal VS, fetal heart rate (FHR), contraction pattern. IV piggyback into a mainline, ALWAYS on an infusion pump.
nursing.administration: Titrate per protocol to adequate labor; never leave unmonitored.
nursing.monitor: Contractions — frequency, duration, intensity, and RESTING TONE — and continuous FHR. Watch for tachysystole (>5 contractions/10 min) and non-reassuring FHR. Prolonged use: water intoxication (oxytocin is antidiuretic) — headache, confusion, hyponatremia. Postpartum: fundus and lochia.
nursing.holdNotify: STOP the infusion for tachysystole or non-reassuring FHR; reposition, fluids, O2 per protocol, notify provider immediately.
nursing.teaching: Explain the goal contraction pattern and why continuous monitoring is needed.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

keyActions: Ask about penicillin allergy before the first dose
Give around the clock; finish the full course

nursing.before: Allergy history (penicillin/cephalosporin); obtain cultures before the first dose if ordered.
nursing.administration: PO with or without food; space doses evenly.
nursing.monitor: Hypersensitivity (rash, wheeze, anaphylaxis), diarrhea (C. difficile), superinfection (thrush).
nursing.holdNotify: Rash, facial/airway swelling, severe watery diarrhea.
nursing.teaching: Finish the entire course; report rash or watery diarrhea; take at evenly spaced times.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 625 mg PO q8h or 1 g q12h
keyActions: Give at the start of a meal to cut GI upset

nursing.administration: Take at the start of a meal to reduce GI upset/diarrhea.
nursing.monitor: GI upset/diarrhea (clavulanate), hypersensitivity, LFTs with prolonged use.
nursing.holdNotify: Severe diarrhea, rash, jaundice.
nursing.teaching: Take with food; finish the course; report watery diarrhea.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 500 mg PO day 1, then 250 mg daily ×4 (or 500 mg daily)
keyActions: Check other QT drugs / low K+/Mg

nursing.monitor: QT prolongation/arrhythmia (esp. with other QT drugs, low K+/Mg), GI upset, LFTs.
nursing.holdNotify: Palpitations/syncope, severe diarrhea.
nursing.teaching: Report palpitations; finish the course; GI upset is common.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 250–750 mg PO q12h; IV 400 mg q8–12h

nursing.administration: Do NOT give with dairy, antacids, or iron/zinc (chelation) — separate ≥2 h. IV over 60 min.
nursing.monitor: Tendon pain/rupture (elderly, steroids), QT prolongation, CNS effects, blood glucose, C. difficile.
nursing.holdNotify: Tendon pain, palpitations, confusion/seizure, severe diarrhea.
nursing.teaching: Avoid strong sun (photosensitivity); report tendon/joint pain; hydrate; separate from antacids/dairy.
nursing.contraindication: Caution: pregnancy/children, myasthenia gravis, aortic aneurysm risk.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 500 mg PO/IV q8h (anaerobes, C. difficile, protozoa)
keyActions: NO alcohol during + 3 days after (disulfiram reaction)
Give with food for GI upset

nursing.administration: With food; IV over 30–60 min.
nursing.monitor: GI upset, metallic taste, peripheral neuropathy/CNS with prolonged use, dark urine (harmless).
nursing.holdNotify: Numbness/tingling, seizures, severe rash.
nursing.teaching: Absolutely no alcohol during and 3 days after (severe flushing/vomiting); metallic taste and darker urine are expected.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 500 mg PO BID–TID with meals; max ~2–2.5 g/day
keyActions: Hold 48 h around IV contrast (lactic acidosis/renal)
Monitor renal function; no hypoglycemia when used alone

nursing.monitor: Renal function (eGFR), GI tolerance, rare lactic acidosis (malaise, myalgia, dyspnea), B12 with long-term use.
nursing.holdNotify: Signs of lactic acidosis; falling eGFR.
nursing.teaching: Take with food; limit alcohol; tell the team before any contrast scan or surgery.
nursing.contraindication: Significant renal impairment, acute illness/dehydration, contrast procedures.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Subcut once daily, individualized basal dose — do NOT give IV
keyActions: Subcut only — never IV; rotate sites
Do NOT mix with other insulins in a syringe

nursing.administration: Subcut at the same time daily; rotate sites; long-acting — never IV; do not mix with other insulins.
nursing.monitor: Blood glucose, hypoglycemia (esp. missed meals), injection sites (lipohypertrophy).
nursing.holdNotify: Hypoglycemia; NPO status without orders.
nursing.teaching: Same time daily; recognize/treat hypoglycemia (15 g fast carbs); rotate sites; don't mix with other insulin.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 40–320 mg PO daily with breakfast (MR forms once daily)
keyActions: Give with breakfast — HYPOGLYCEMIA risk (skipped meals/elderly)

nursing.monitor: Blood glucose, hypoglycemia (unlike metformin, sulfonylureas can cause it), weight.
nursing.holdNotify: Hypoglycemia; NPO without a coverage plan.
nursing.teaching: Eat regular meals; carry fast sugar; recognize hypoglycemia; limit alcohol.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Individualized PO once daily on an empty stomach
keyActions: Empty stomach, same time AM; wait 30–60 min before food

nursing.administration: Morning, empty stomach, 30–60 min before breakfast; keep the same brand.
nursing.monitor: TSH/T4 per schedule, HR/weight, signs of over- (palpitations, heat intolerance) or under-replacement.
nursing.holdNotify: Chest pain, palpitations, tremor.
nursing.teaching: Lifelong, same time daily, empty stomach; separate from calcium/iron by 4 h; don't switch brands without checking.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Indication-specific (e.g., 4–10 mg IV/PO); taper if prolonged
keyActions: Never stop a long course abruptly — taper
Infection signs may be masked; give with food

nursing.monitor: Blood glucose (hyperglycemia even in non-diabetics), BP, K+, infection (masked), mood/sleep, GI bleeding with prolonged use.
nursing.holdNotify: Persistent hyperglycemia, GI bleeding, signs of infection.
nursing.teaching: Take with food; never stop long courses abruptly; report black stools or mood changes; medical-alert ID if chronic.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 25–100 mg PO daily

nursing.monitor: BP, K+ (hyperkalemia), renal function, dizziness.
nursing.holdNotify: Symptomatic hypotension, hyperkalemia, facial/airway swelling.
nursing.teaching: Rise slowly; avoid potassium salt substitutes; pregnancy is contraindicated; don't stop abruptly.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 10–80 mg PO once daily

nursing.monitor: Muscle pain/weakness (myopathy/rhabdo — check CK if symptomatic), LFTs, lipid panel.
nursing.holdNotify: Unexplained muscle pain, dark urine, jaundice.
nursing.teaching: Report muscle aches/dark urine; avoid grapefruit juice; pregnancy contraindicated.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 12.5–25 mg PO daily in the morning
keyActions: Give in the morning; monitor K+ (low), glucose & uric acid

nursing.monitor: K+ (hypokalemia), Na+, glucose, uric acid (gout), BP/orthostasis, renal function.
nursing.holdNotify: Low K+, symptomatic hypotension, gout flare.
nursing.teaching: Take in the morning; potassium-rich foods unless restricted; rise slowly; sun protection.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: PO per order; sublingual for acute angina
keyActions: Headache common; check BP & hold if low

nursing.monitor: BP, HR, headache (common), chest-pain relief.
nursing.holdNotify: SBP below floor, worsening hypotension/syncope.
nursing.teaching: Sit when taking; treat headache with acetaminophen; never combine with erectile-dysfunction drugs; keep a nitrate-free period.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 75 mg PO daily (300–600 mg loading per protocol)
keyActions: Do NOT stop before procedures without advice (stent thrombosis)

nursing.monitor: Bleeding (stool, gums, bruising), Hgb/Hct.
nursing.holdNotify: Active bleeding, melena.
nursing.teaching: Don't stop abruptly — confirm with cardiology; report unusual bleeding; tell dentists/surgeons.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 15–20 mg PO daily WITH food (indication-specific)
keyActions: Take with food (20 mg) for absorption
Bleeding precautions; don't double up a missed dose

nursing.monitor: Bleeding (no routine level needed), renal function, Hgb.
nursing.holdNotify: Active bleeding; spinal/epidural procedures (hematoma risk).
nursing.teaching: Take with the evening meal; don't stop abruptly; report bleeding/black stools; carry an anticoagulant alert.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 50–100 mg PO q4–6h PRN; max 400 mg/day

nursing.monitor: Pain, RR/sedation, seizures (lowers threshold), serotonin syndrome (agitation, clonus, fever) with other serotonergics.
nursing.holdNotify: Oversedation, RR<12, seizure, serotonin syndrome.
nursing.teaching: No alcohol; caution driving; report tremor/agitation; don't exceed the max.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: IV/IM 15–30 mg q6h; max 5 days total
keyActions: Limit to ≤5 days (GI bleed, renal, bleeding)

nursing.monitor: GI bleeding (black stools), renal function/urine output, bleeding/bruising, BP.
nursing.holdNotify: GI bleeding, oliguria, rising creatinine.
nursing.teaching: Short-term only; report black stools or decreased urine; avoid other NSAIDs/alcohol.
nursing.contraindication: Active GI bleed, renal impairment, bleeding risk, peri-op CABG.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Procedural sedation: titrate small IV doses per protocol

nursing.before: Continuous SpO2, RR, BP; suction, oxygen, bag-valve-mask, and flumazenil available; verify NPO.
nursing.administration: Titrate small IV increments slowly; potentiates opioids.
nursing.monitor: Respiratory depression/apnea, sedation depth, BP.
nursing.holdNotify: RR<12, apnea, hypotension, oversedation.
nursing.teaching: Amnesia for the procedure is expected; no driving 24 h; arrange an escort.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Agitation/delirium: 0.5–5 mg PO/IM per order

nursing.monitor: QT/ECG, extrapyramidal symptoms (rigidity, dystonia, restlessness), neuroleptic malignant syndrome (fever, rigidity, altered mental status), sedation.
nursing.holdNotify: QT prolongation, EPS, fever + rigidity (NMS).
nursing.teaching: Report stiffness, restlessness, or fever; rise slowly; avoid alcohol.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Neb 500 mcg q6–8h; often combined with salbutamol
keyActions: Keep mist out of the eyes (mydriasis)

nursing.monitor: Lung sounds, respiratory effort, HR; dry mouth.
nursing.holdNotify: Paradoxical bronchospasm, eye pain/blurred vision.
nursing.teaching: Rinse mouth; protect eyes from the mist (esp. glaucoma); it's maintenance, not the fastest rescue.
nursing.contraindication: Caution in narrow-angle glaucoma, BPH/urinary retention.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 4–8 mg PO/IV q8h PRN nausea
keyActions: Monitor ECG — QT prolongation (push IV slowly)
Check other QT drugs / low K+/Mg

nursing.monitor: QT prolongation (dilute/push IV slowly), constipation, headache.
nursing.holdNotify: Palpitations/syncope.
nursing.teaching: Report palpitations; headache/constipation are common; ODT melts on the tongue.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 20–40 mg PO/IV daily–BID

nursing.monitor: Symptom relief; renal function (dose-adjust); rare confusion in elderly/renal.
nursing.holdNotify: Persistent symptoms, black/tarry stools (possible bleed).
nursing.teaching: May take with or without food; report black stools; separate from antacids by ~1 h.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: 500 mg PO/IV BID, titrated (IV over 15 min)

nursing.monitor: Seizure frequency, mood/behavior changes (irritability, depression, rare suicidality), drowsiness, renal function.
nursing.holdNotify: Mood/behavior change, increased seizures.
nursing.teaching: Take consistently; never stop abruptly; report mood changes; caution driving until stable.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Severe metabolic acidosis / hyperkalemia / some overdoses: per ABG/protocol
keyActions: Dose by ABG/protocol; flush line (precipitates with calcium)

nursing.monitor: ABG/pH, K+ (falls as acidosis corrects), Na+, fluid overload/hypernatremia, IV site (irritant).
nursing.holdNotify: Overcorrection (alkalosis), low K+, infiltration.
nursing.teaching: Used for specific emergencies; flush the line separately from calcium-containing drugs.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)

drugs.doseSummary: Severe hypoglycemia: 1 mg IM/SC/IV; beta-blocker overdose per protocol
keyActions: Give IM/SC when no IV access for hypoglycemia
Turn patient on the side (vomiting); recheck glucose
Give carbs once awake — effect is brief

nursing.monitor: Blood glucose, level of consciousness, nausea/vomiting.
nursing.holdNotify: No response in ~15 min (needs IV dextrose).
nursing.teaching: Position on the side (may vomit); eat carbs once alert; teach family to use the home kit.
nursing.source: per FDA labeling (DailyMed) & Open RN Nursing Pharmacology (CC BY)
